data.title: Birding for Beginners
data.description:
Whether you’ve traveled the world with binoculars or simply paused to think, “Hey, that’s a cool bird!” — congratulations, you’re already a birder! Now’s your chance to build the skills to confidently identify and find the birds you love, from flashy migrants to the feathered friends right outside your window.

Join instructor Nick Dujnic for a welcoming, hands-on introduction to birding. We’ll spend the first hour in the “classroom” learning core skills like field marks, bird families, behavior clues, and listening basics. Then we’ll head outside for an hour of real-world birding to put your new knowledge into practice.

Everyone is welcome—bring your curiosity, your questions, and your sense of wonder. Let’s go find some birds together!

### Program Registration & Waitlist Policy

IMPORTANT: Please read the following carefully to ensure you are fully registered.

You are officially registered only if you receive a confirmation email from Mobilize stating your status as “Registered.” Shortly before the event, you will receive a notice to "Confirm" your registration.

If you are on the waitlist, you are not registered unless you receive a separate notification informing you that you have been moved from the waitlist to registered status.

If you have not received a “Registered” confirmation email at least 24 hours prior to the event, you did not secure a spot in the program. Please do not come by anticipating no-shows.

If you are registered, please continue to monitor your email for any last-minute updates. Unforeseen circumstances, including weather or other factors, may result in schedule changes or cancellations.

We appreciate your understanding and look forward to seeing you in the park!

data.title: Guided Nature Walk | Spring Migration
data.description:
*This walk will run from April - June*

Each spring, Seward Park becomes a lively stopover for birds traveling north along the Pacific Flyway. Join us for a relaxed and curious guided walk focused on the migrants passing through, from colorful warblers and energetic swallows to thrushes, vireos, and other travelers returning to their breeding grounds as the days grow longer.

Over the course of this 2-hour walk (covering less than 2 miles), naturalist and guide Nick Dujnic will take you on an exploration of Seward Park’s old-growth forest, lakeside habitat, and open waters—scanning treetops, listening for fresh spring songs, and watching for movement as birds refuel on their journey north.

Come experience the energy, color, and movement of spring migration—and meet the birds just passing through. Binoculars will be provided for those that don't have them

### Program Registration & Waitlist Policy

IMPORTANT: Please read the following carefully to ensure you are fully registered.

You are officially registered only if you receive a confirmation email from Mobilize stating your status as “Registered.” Shortly before the event, you will receive a notice to "Confirm" your registration.

If you are on the waitlist, you are not registered unless you receive a separate notification informing you that you have been moved from the waitlist to registered status.

If you have not received a “Registered” confirmation email at least 24 hours prior to the event, you did not secure a spot in the program. Please do not come by anticipating no-shows.

If you are registered, please continue to monitor your email for any last-minute updates. Unforeseen circumstances, including weather or other factors, may result in schedule changes or cancellations.

We appreciate your understanding and look forward to seeing you in the park!
data.accessibility_notes: We will be walking approximately 2 miles at a moderate pace. Some areas may have uneven terrain and mild steepness.

data.title: Community Science | BAT Leader Training 2026
data.description:
Have you ever seen bats overhead on a warm summer evening? Did you know a single bat can consume 50% of its body weight in mosquitos and other insects each night? Ready to make contributions as a community scientist leader? Woodland Park Zoo and Bats Northwest will facilitate this Bat Activity Trends (BAT) Volunteer Leader training that will enable you to conduct bat activity observations on your own, as well as lead others in conducting observations.
 
Through this BAT Leader training you will learn natural history information about bats (and Washington bats specifically) and how to gather important research information by estimating the level of bat activity observed for a thirty-minute period after sunset. By completing this training, you will join Seward Park Audubon Center and/or City of Mercer Island Parks as a volunteer BAT observer and BAT Leader. Details about your summer volunteer expectations will be provided by your respective volunteer coordinator.

data.title: Molly Hashimoto | The West: An Artist's View
data.description:
*This is a drop-in exhibit that will run April 3 - June 30. Registration is not required*

Molly Hashimoto’s art is an appreciation of the great beauty of the western United States, its magnificent landscapes, stately trees, and colorful birds, butterflies and wildflowers. The exhibit will include relief prints, watercolors and etchings.

[Visit Molly's Website](https://www.mollyhashimoto.com/)

### About the Artist:

Seattle resident Molly Hashimoto's paintings and prints have been published for over 30 years as notecards, holiday cards, calendars and children’s books by Pomegranate Communications.   Her block prints have been published in many calendars and notecard collections. New cards and calendars for 2027 will be released in 2026.

Molly's books, published by Mountaineers Books, include: Colors of the West: An Artist’s Guide to Nature’s Palette, Mt. Rainier National Park: An Artist’s Tour, Birds of the West: An Artist’s Guide, and Trees of the West: An Artist’s Guide, which received an Award of Excellence for Botanical Art and Illustration from The Council on Botanical and Horticultural Libraries. Her latest book, Wildflowers of the West: An Artist’s Guide was released in fall 2025.  She has has annually exhibited her work in solo shows at the Elisabeth C. Miller Library at the University of Washington's Center for Urban Horticulture, most recently in 2025.

Teaching is an important part of her artistic journey; she offers annual outdoor seminars at Sitka Center for Art and Ecology at Cascade Head in Oregon, the North Cascades Institute in North Cascades National Park, and Yosemite Conservancy in Yosemite National Park. For many years she has offered library programs around Puget Sound, sharing her appreciation for nature with the public.

data.title: Guided Nature Walk | Trees as Teachers: Learning from the Forest

location.venue: Lost Lake Trailhead near Picnic Shelter 3

data.description:
In the Pacific Northwest, trees shape the world around us they create the air we breathe, cool and filter our waters, shelter wildlife, and influence the very climate we live in. From towering Douglas firs to resilient madronas along the shoreline, these trees are essential to the health of our ecosystems and communities.

Join Brianna Klein, Public Programs Manager, for a slow, intentional walk through the forest as we deepen our relationship with some of the Pacific Northwest’s most iconic trees Douglas fir, Western red cedar, bigleaf maple, madrona, Garry oak, and alder. Rooted in the Tend, Gather, Grow curriculum and teachings, this experience invites us to learn from the land through relationship, reciprocity, and care. Together, we’ll come to understand the ways these trees survive how they adapt, collaborate, and remain resilient through changing conditions and reflect on what we can learn from them. Using the art of noticing, we’ll engage our senses to observe textures, scents, shapes, and the subtle details that bring each tree to life.

This experience is more than tree identification. Through shared stories, ecological knowledge, and teachings, we’ll learn how trees collaborate, adapt, and give generously to the ecosystems and communities around them and reflect on how these lessons can guide us in our own lives.

### Program Registration & Waitlist Policy

IMPORTANT: Please read the following carefully to ensure you are fully registered.

You are officially registered only if you receive a confirmation email from Mobilize stating your status as “Registered.” Shortly before the event, you will receive a notice to "Confirm" your registration.

If you are on the waitlist, you are not registered unless you receive a separate notification informing you that you have been moved from the waitlist to registered status.

If you have not received a “Registered” confirmation email at least 24 hours prior to the event, you did not secure a spot in the program. Please do not come by anticipating no-shows.

If you are registered, please continue to monitor your email for any last-minute updates. Unforeseen circumstances, including weather or other factors, may result in schedule changes or cancellations.

We appreciate your understanding and look forward to seeing you in the park!
data.accessibility_notes: This walk is designed to be welcoming and accessible to a wide range of participants. The route will be approximately 1–2 miles following a relatively easy pace with frequent stops for observation, reflection, and rest. We will remain on established paths whenever possible, though some surfaces may be uneven. Participants are encouraged to engage in ways that feel comfortable to them whether that’s through movement, quiet observation, or sensory exploration. We invite you to bring any supports you may need, such as mobility aids, water, or seating. If you have specific access needs or questions ahead of time, please reach out so we can do our best to support your experience.

data.title: Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez | Urban Forest and Heat Islands
data.description:
### Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez

As seasons grow hotter and climate impacts become more visible, understanding the role of urban tree canopy is more important than ever. Here in South Seattle, the reduced access to shade stands in contrast to other areas of the city, leaving local residents especially vulnerable to the impacts of climate change. 

Ed Dominguez is a passionate Seattle-based naturalist, renowned bird expert, local educator, trusted voice in conservation, and former member of our Audubon staff. His research on trees and his understanding of the effects of climate change highlight the importance of urban tree canopy. Ed also understands the issues and politics that have brought us to our current situation and shows us how we can all play a part in creating cooler, healthier, and more resilient communities.
 
Seward Park Audubon Center is excited to team up with our beloved partner in conservation for a three-part series of presentations designed to educate, inform, and rally our friends and neighbors to take action on this critical issue of trees and climate. Each of these free public events will take place at the Rainer Art Center in Columbia City, or you can participate live on the Seward Park Audubon YouTube channel.

### Urban Forest and Heat Islands

In this first program, Ed Dominguez invites South Seattle community members to explore how urban forests shape the health and resilience of our neighborhoods.  Through his deep knowledge of the natural world and passion for connecting people to place, Ed will provide important insights into the many benefits of the urban tree canopy, from cooling our streets and improving air quality to supporting wildlife and community well-being.
The session will also examine the history and inequities of tree canopy distribution, highlighting why some neighborhoods experience more extreme heat than others. Through this lens, we’ll explore urban heat islands and share practical, community-centered strategies for creating cooler, greener, and more equitable neighborhoods.

Check our calendar to learn about additional programs in this series.

*This event is sponsored by: Washington Department of Natural Resources*
data.accessibility_notes: Rainier Arts Center is fully ADA accessible. It features accessible entrances, an outdoor wheelchair lift, ADA-compliant restrooms on both levels, and designated wheelchair seating in the auditorium.

data.title: Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez | Saving Trees and Protecting Urban Forests
data.description:
### Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez
As seasons grow hotter and climate impacts become more visible, understanding the role of urban tree canopy is more important than ever. Here in South Seattle, the reduced access to shade stands in contrast to other areas of the city, leaving local residents especially vulnerable to the impacts of climate change. 

Ed Dominguez is a passionate Seattle-based naturalist, renowned bird expert, local educator, trusted voice in conservation, and former member of our Audubon staff. His research on trees and his understanding of the effects of climate change highlight the importance of urban tree canopy. Ed also understands the issues and politics that have brought us to our current situation and shows us how we can all play a part in creating cooler, healthier, and more resilient communities. 

Seward Park Audubon Center is excited to team up with our beloved partner in conservation for a three-part series of presentations designed to educate, inform, and rally our friends and neighbors to take action on this critical issue of trees and climate. Each of these free public events will take place at the Rainer Art Center in Columbia City, or you can participate live on the Seward Park Audubon YouTube channel.

### Saving Trees and Protecting Urban Forests
Seattle’s urban forests are facing mounting pressures, from development and extreme heat to changing policy and limited protections. As climate change accelerates, the need to preserve, restore, and expand our tree canopy has never been more urgent.

In this second installment of our Urban Forest Climate Series, Ed Dominguez takes a closer look at the challenges trees face in our urban environment and the critical actions needed to protect them. With his deep knowledge of local ecosystems and conservation policy, Ed explores the forces behind tree loss in South Seattle and across the city, while offering practical insight into how communities can advocate for stronger protections and stewardship.

Through a combination of science, lived experience, and community-based solutions, this talk will empower participants with the tools and knowledge to make a difference from supporting equitable tree canopy initiatives to engaging in local decision-making processes that shape the future of our urban forest.

Join us as we move from awareness to action and learn how we can all play a role in preserving the trees that sustain healthier, cooler, and more resilient neighborhoods.

Check our calendar to learn about additional programs in this series.

*This event is sponsored by: Washington Department of Natural Resources*
data.accessibility_notes: Notes from the organizer: Rainier Arts Center is fully ADA accessible. It features accessible entrances, an outdoor wheelchair lift, ADA-compliant restrooms on both levels, and designated wheelchair seating in the auditorium.

data.title: Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez | Urban Forests and The Birds That Need Them
data.description:
### Urban Forest Climate Series with Ed Dominguez
As seasons grow hotter and climate impacts become more visible, understanding the role of urban tree canopy is more important than ever. Here in South Seattle, the reduced access to shade stands in contrast to other areas of the city, leaving local residents especially vulnerable to the impacts of climate change. 

Ed Dominguez is a passionate Seattle-based naturalist, renowned bird expert, local educator, trusted voice in conservation, and former member of our Audubon staff. His research on trees and his understanding of the effects of climate change highlight the importance of urban tree canopy. Ed also understands the issues and politics that have brought us to our current situation and shows us how we can all play a part in creating cooler, healthier, and more resilient communities. 

Seward Park Audubon Center is excited to team up with our beloved partner in conservation for a three-part series of presentations designed to educate, inform, and rally our friends and neighbors to take action on this critical issue of trees and climate. Each of these free public events will take place at the Rainer Art Center in Columbia City, or you can participate live on the Seward Park Audubon YouTube channel.

### Urban Forests and The Birds That Need Them
Urban forests are more than shade and greenery, they are vital habitat for birds and other wildlife that depend on trees for survival. As climate change and development reshape our city, these habitats are shrinking, with serious consequences for the species that rely on them.

In this final installment of our Urban Forest Climate Series, Ed Dominguez brings his expertise as a renowned birder and naturalist to explore the deep connection between urban tree canopy and bird life. Drawing on his years of observation and research, Ed highlights how trees support migratory and resident birds alike, and how the loss of canopy disproportionately impacts biodiversity, especially in areas like South Seattle.

Participants will gain a better understanding of which birds depend on urban forests, how climate change is affecting their behavior and survival, and what we can do locally to protect and restore the habitats they need. From planting and caring for trees to advocating for bird-friendly policies and spaces, this talk offers meaningful ways for individuals and communities to support the wildlife that make our city vibrant and alive.

Join us to discover how protecting urban forests also means protecting the birds that define and enrich our shared environment and how, together, we can help ensure they continue to thrive.

*This event is sponsored by: Washington Department of Natural Resources*
data.accessibility_notes: Notes from the organizer: Rainier Arts Center is fully ADA accessible. It features accessible entrances, an outdoor wheelchair lift, ADA-compliant restrooms on both levels, and designated wheelchair seating in the auditorium.

data.title: Techno-Naturalist Workshop
data.description:
Did you know the supercomputer in your pocket can help you unlock the secrets of the natural world? Drop in anytime for our Techno-Naturalist Workshop, an open-house event designed to help you use your smartphone to explore, identify, and connect more deeply with the nature around you.

No registration needed—just bring your device and your curiosity! Our experts will be on hand to answer questions and give hands-on guidance with powerful community science tools like Merlin, eBird, Audubon, and iNaturalist. Learn how these apps help you identify birds and wildlife, track your sightings, and join a global network of people contributing to real scientific understanding.

Whether you're brand-new to nature apps or looking to level up your skills, this is the perfect chance to turn your phone into a nature-detecting super tool. Come discover how technology can make every walk in the woods feel a little more magical.

data.title: Guided Nature Walk | Leaves in Light: Printing Nature, Inspiring Care

location.venue: Lost Lake Trailhead near Picnic Shelter 3 Lost Lake Trail, Seattle, WA 98118

data.description:
Discover the forest through art in Leaves in Light, a hands-on workshop that blends cyanotype printing, plant identification, and conservation connection. Using sunlight and natural materials, participants create striking blue prints while exploring the shapes, patterns, and stories of plants found in the local landscape.

Guided by Center staff, participants engage in a slow, intentional walk to observe and identify common forest species, learning to recognize plants through form, texture, and habitat. They then gather fallen or abundant materials to compose their own cyanotype prints, using art as a way to interpret and reflect on the ecosystem around them.

Throughout the experience, art becomes a tool for deeper attention and understanding. By noticing more closely, participants build a stronger sense of connection to place and are invited to consider their role in caring for it. The program concludes with reflection and simple, accessible ways to support local conservation efforts.

No prior experience in art or plant identification is needed, just curiosity and a willingness to look closely.

### Program Registration & Waitlist Policy

IMPORTANT: Please read the following carefully to ensure you are fully registered.

You are officially registered only if you receive a confirmation email from Mobilize stating your status as “Registered.” Shortly before the event, you will receive a notice to "Confirm" your registration.

If you are on the waitlist, you are not registered unless you receive a separate notification informing you that you have been moved from the waitlist to registered status.

If you have not received a “Registered” confirmation email at least 24 hours prior to the event, you did not secure a spot in the program. Please do not come by anticipating no-shows.

If you are registered, please continue to monitor your email for any last-minute updates. Unforeseen circumstances, including weather or other factors, may result in schedule changes or cancellations.

We appreciate your understanding and look forward to seeing you in the park!
data.accessibility_notes: This program includes a slow-paced walk of less than one mile on forested trails. The route may include uneven terrain, such as dirt paths, roots, or small elevation changes. Participants are encouraged to move at their own pace, and frequent stops are built into the experience for observation, discussion, and art-making. If you have specific access needs or questions, we welcome conversation in advance to help make the experience as inclusive and comfortable as possible.
